Object detection in remote sensing images has become a focal point in computer vision research. With the advancement of remote sensing technology, there is an increasing demand for detecting objects at arbitrary orientations. However, objects in remote sensing images often exhibit diverse scales and random rotations. Furthermore, rotated objects typically have high aspect ratios, making bounding box predictions sensitive to angular variations and increasing the complexity of detection. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an improved rotated object detection model based on geometric and size relationships. Firstly, an improved ResNet50 network based on multi-scale perception enhancement is proposed, where a feature pyramid network (FPN) and a channel attention mechanism are combined to enhance object features. Then, a coarse-to-fine optimization strategy is employed to precisely detect the rotated object. In the coarse stage, the rotated angle is computed by predicting the geometric relationship between the center points and boundary center points, which reduces the issues of angle periodicity and edge exchangeability. In the refined stage, an adaptive rotated intersection-over-union (IoU) loss is proposed, which incorporates the object’s aspect ratio and area, significantly improving the model’s adaptability across various scales, particularly excelling in high-aspect-ratio objects. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model effectively identifies rotated objects and outperforms existing models.

item.title: Unified Pix Token And Word Token Generative Language Model
item.dc:creator: Haun Leung
ZiNan Wang
item.description: Since the emergence of Vision Transformer (ViT), it has been widely used in generative language model and generative visual model. Especially in the current state-of-art open source multimodal models, ViT obtained by CLIP or SigLIP method serves as the vision encoder backbone to help them acquire visual understanding capabilities. But this method leads to limitations in visual understanding for details, such as difficulty in recognizing small text or numbers in images. To address these issues, we propose a new model to unify pix token and word token into the generative language model. The new model also features with each pix of image having its own token embedding, color folding, global conditional attention approximation and image unsupervised pretraining. We conducted image unsupervised pretraining experiments using our new model to explore its potential. The experimental results show that it has good performance even in small model and with limited training data. We believe our model also conforms to the scaling law, as long as model parameters and training data increased, its performance will continue to improve. Published: 2026-05-13T18:38:51+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haun Leung; ZiNan Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Since the emergence of Vision Transformer (ViT), it has been widely used in generative language model and generative visual model. Especially in the current state-of-art open source multimodal models, ViT obtained by CLIP or SigLIP method serves as the vision encoder backbone to help them acquire visual understanding capabilities. But this method leads to limitations in visual understanding for details, such as difficulty in recognizing small text or numbers in images. To address these issues, we propose a new model to unify pix token and word token into the generative language model. The new model also features with each pix of image having its own token embedding, color folding, global conditional attention approximation and image unsupervised pretraining. We conducted image unsupervised pretraining experiments using our new model to explore its potential. The experimental results show that it has good performance even in small model and with limited training data. We believe our model also conforms to the scaling law, as long as model parameters and training data increased, its performance will continue to improve.

item.title: OmniLiDAR: A Unified Diffusion Framework for Multi-Domain 3D LiDAR Generation
item.dc:creator: Youquan Liu
Weidong Yang
Ao Liang
Xiang Xu
Lingdong Kong
Yang Wu
Dekai Zhu
Xin Li
Runnan Chen
Ben Fei
Tongliang Liu
Wanli Ouyang
item.description: LiDAR scene generation is increasingly important for scalable simulation and synthetic data creation, especially under diverse sensing conditions that are costly to capture at scale. Typically, diffusion-based LiDAR generators are developed under single-domain settings, requiring separate models for different datasets or sensing conditions and hindering unified, controllable synthesis under heterogeneous distribution shifts. To this end, we present OmniLiDAR, a unified text-conditioned diffusion framework that generates LiDAR scans in a shared range-image representation across eight representative domains spanning three shift types: adverse weather, sensor-configuration changes (e.g., reduced beams), and cross-platform acquisition (vehicle, drone, and quadruped). To enable training a single model over heterogeneous domains without isolating optimization by domain, we introduce a Cross-Domain Training Strategy (CDTS) that mixes domains within each mini-batch and leverages conditioning to steer generation. We further propose Cross-Domain Feature Modeling (CDFM), which captures directional dependencies along azimuth and elevation axes to reflect the anisotropic scanning structure of range images, and Domain-Adaptive Feature Scaling (DAFS) as a lightweight modulation to account for structured domain-dependent feature shifts during denoising. In the absence of a public consolidated benchmark, we construct an 8-domain dataset by combining real-world scans with physically based weather simulation and systematic beam reduction while following official splits. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong generation fidelity and consistent gains in downstream use cases, including generative data augmentation for LiDAR semantic segmentation and 3D object detection, as well as robustness evaluation under corruptions, with consistent benefits in limited-label regimes. Published: 2026-05-13T17:42:20+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.781 (must_read)

Authors: Youquan Liu; Weidong Yang; Ao Liang; Xiang Xu; Lingdong Kong; Yang Wu; Dekai Zhu; Xin Li; Runnan Chen; Ben Fei; Tongliang Liu; Wanli Ouyang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

LiDAR scene generation is increasingly important for scalable simulation and synthetic data creation, especially under diverse sensing conditions that are costly to capture at scale. Typically, diffusion-based LiDAR generators are developed under single-domain settings, requiring separate models for different datasets or sensing conditions and hindering unified, controllable synthesis under heterogeneous distribution shifts. To this end, we present OmniLiDAR, a unified text-conditioned diffusion framework that generates LiDAR scans in a shared range-image representation across eight representative domains spanning three shift types: adverse weather, sensor-configuration changes (e.g., reduced beams), and cross-platform acquisition (vehicle, drone, and quadruped). To enable training a single model over heterogeneous domains without isolating optimization by domain, we introduce a Cross-Domain Training Strategy (CDTS) that mixes domains within each mini-batch and leverages conditioning to steer generation. We further propose Cross-Domain Feature Modeling (CDFM), which captures directional dependencies along azimuth and elevation axes to reflect the anisotropic scanning structure of range images, and Domain-Adaptive Feature Scaling (DAFS) as a lightweight modulation to account for structured domain-dependent feature shifts during denoising. In the absence of a public consolidated benchmark, we construct an 8-domain dataset by combining real-world scans with physically based weather simulation and systematic beam reduction while following official splits. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong generation fidelity and consistent gains in downstream use cases, including generative data augmentation for LiDAR semantic segmentation and 3D object detection, as well as robustness evaluation under corruptions, with consistent benefits in limited-label regimes.

item.title: DB-CRNet: A novel weakly supervised framework for Cross-Resolution land cover classification
item.dc:creator: Shaoyu Wang
Shuo Chen
Yayang Lu
Yanling Long
Ming Yin
Ying Sun
Dongmei Yan
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation
item.description: High spatial resolution land cover products play a crucial role in urban planning and renewal by providing detailed spatial information for decision-making. However, generating these products through fully supervised classification approaches requires extensive pixel-level annotations, which are time-consuming and costly. Weakly supervised methods offer a practical alternative by using low spatial resolution land cover products as coarse labels. Nevertheless, the mismatch in resolution introduces significant label noise and blurred boundaries, limiting classification accuracy. To address these challenges, this study proposes a Dual-Branch Cross-Resolution Network (DB-CRNet) that uses only low spatial resolution labels and high spatial resolution images for land cover classification. DB-CRNet adopts a non-downsampling dual-branch framework, consisting of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) branch, a Wavelet Visual State Space Model (WVSSM) branch, and a Label-Filtering Dual-Branch Hybrid Loss (LFDB-Loss) module. This design enables effective cross-resolution learning while preserving fine spatial details. Experimental results show that DB-CRNet outperforms existing benchmark models (e.g., Paraformer, L2HNet), achieving an mIoU value of 72.46% on the public Chesapeake dataset and 63.48% on the self-built Guilin dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed network exhibits strong generalization across different data sources and spatial resolutions. Overall, this study provides an efficient and economic method for high spatial resolution land cover mapping using low spatial resolution coarse labels, significantly reducing the labeling cost and ensuring the precision of classification results. Published: 2026-05-15T16:20:21+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shaoyu Wang; Shuo Chen; Yayang Lu; Yanling Long; Ming Yin; Ying Sun; Dongmei Yan

Journal: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation

DOI: 10.1016/j.jag.2026.105332

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

High spatial resolution land cover products play a crucial role in urban planning and renewal by providing detailed spatial information for decision-making. However, generating these products through fully supervised classification approaches requires extensive pixel-level annotations, which are time-consuming and costly. Weakly supervised methods offer a practical alternative by using low spatial resolution land cover products as coarse labels. Nevertheless, the mismatch in resolution introduces significant label noise and blurred boundaries, limiting classification accuracy. To address these challenges, this study proposes a Dual-Branch Cross-Resolution Network (DB-CRNet) that uses only low spatial resolution labels and high spatial resolution images for land cover classification. DB-CRNet adopts a non-downsampling dual-branch framework, consisting of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) branch, a Wavelet Visual State Space Model (WVSSM) branch, and a Label-Filtering Dual-Branch Hybrid Loss (LFDB-Loss) module. This design enables effective cross-resolution learning while preserving fine spatial details. Experimental results show that DB-CRNet outperforms existing benchmark models (e.g., Paraformer, L2HNet), achieving an mIoU value of 72.46% on the public Chesapeake dataset and 63.48% on the self-built Guilin dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed network exhibits strong generalization across different data sources and spatial resolutions. Overall, this study provides an efficient and economic method for high spatial resolution land cover mapping using low spatial resolution coarse labels, significantly reducing the labeling cost and ensuring the precision of classification results.

item.title: An adaptive method for ocean front detection using spatial–frequency fusion and uncertainty-aware attention
item.dc:creator: Le Gao
Yi Yang
Nan Liu
Mengjiao Wang
Xiaofeng Li
item.description: Ocean fronts are sharp physical and biogeochemical interfaces that organize upper-ocean circulation and ecosystems. Mapping them from satellite imagery remains challenging: classical approaches rely on gradient- or statistic-based criteria and can be sensitive to parameter choices and data characteristics, whereas existing deep learning models often struggle to generalize across products and resolutions. We present an uncertainty-aware, self-supervised framework that fuses spatial and frequency cues with pixel-level confidence weighting derived from uncertainties in satellite products. The method avoids manual thresholds, improves weak-front reliability, and preserves continuity for strong and moderate fronts. In cross-product evaluations against annotated fronts and ship-based observations, it achieves higher precision, recall, intersection-over-union and hit rate performance, while reducing spurious detections in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. Applied globally to long-term multi-satellite SST and chlorophyll-a datasets, the framework generates daily thermal (1981–2022, 0.05°) and chlorophyll-a (1997–2022, 4 km) front maps, providing per-pixel location, intensity, and uncertainty. A case study in the eastern tropical Pacific resolves distinct responses of thermal versus chlorophyll-a fronts to tropical instability waves, the chlorophyll-a-rich tongue, and ENSO phases. This resolution-agnostic approach provides a generalizable basis for robust, uncertainty-aware front detection in satellite remote sensing. Code, pretrained weights, and exemplar products are released to support reuse. Published: 2026-05-15T13:21:24+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Le Gao; Yi Yang; Nan Liu; Mengjiao Wang; Xiaofeng Li

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115483

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Ocean fronts are sharp physical and biogeochemical interfaces that organize upper-ocean circulation and ecosystems. Mapping them from satellite imagery remains challenging: classical approaches rely on gradient- or statistic-based criteria and can be sensitive to parameter choices and data characteristics, whereas existing deep learning models often struggle to generalize across products and resolutions. We present an uncertainty-aware, self-supervised framework that fuses spatial and frequency cues with pixel-level confidence weighting derived from uncertainties in satellite products. The method avoids manual thresholds, improves weak-front reliability, and preserves continuity for strong and moderate fronts. In cross-product evaluations against annotated fronts and ship-based observations, it achieves higher precision, recall, intersection-over-union and hit rate performance, while reducing spurious detections in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. Applied globally to long-term multi-satellite SST and chlorophyll-a datasets, the framework generates daily thermal (1981–2022, 0.05°) and chlorophyll-a (1997–2022, 4 km) front maps, providing per-pixel location, intensity, and uncertainty. A case study in the eastern tropical Pacific resolves distinct responses of thermal versus chlorophyll-a fronts to tropical instability waves, the chlorophyll-a-rich tongue, and ENSO phases. This resolution-agnostic approach provides a generalizable basis for robust, uncertainty-aware front detection in satellite remote sensing. Code, pretrained weights, and exemplar products are released to support reuse.

item.title: Divide and Conquer: Multi-channel Inconsistency Learning Network for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
item.dc:creator: Lisong Ou
Zhixin Li
item.description: Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) aims to model the inconsistency between text and image. Despite significant progress in this field, existing methods are often limited to focusing on single-granularity inconsistencies, while overlooking the multi-granular semantic contradictions inherent in different irony types. Furthermore, how to flexibly integrate multiple tasks to achieve reliable decision-making remains a critical issue that demands urgent exploration. To address these issues, we propose a novel multi-channel inconsistency learning network, which utilizes a divide-and-conquer strategy to enable refined handling of various sarcasm types within multiple channels. Specifically, we first introduce the cross-modal prior knowledge from CLIP to enrich and guide the learning of unimodal features. Subsequently, we construct multi-granular channels to adapt to different types of sarcastic expressions, thereby comprehensively capturing inconsistency information. Furthermore, we specifically design quality-aware multi-channel fusion method to further enhance the reliability of sarcasm detection results. Extensive experiments and in-depth analyses on publicly available benchmark datasets for multimodal sarcasm detection demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Published: 2026-05-16T00:28:34+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lisong Ou; Zhixin Li

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104479

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) aims to model the inconsistency between text and image. Despite significant progress in this field, existing methods are often limited to focusing on single-granularity inconsistencies, while overlooking the multi-granular semantic contradictions inherent in different irony types. Furthermore, how to flexibly integrate multiple tasks to achieve reliable decision-making remains a critical issue that demands urgent exploration. To address these issues, we propose a novel multi-channel inconsistency learning network, which utilizes a divide-and-conquer strategy to enable refined handling of various sarcasm types within multiple channels. Specifically, we first introduce the cross-modal prior knowledge from CLIP to enrich and guide the learning of unimodal features. Subsequently, we construct multi-granular channels to adapt to different types of sarcastic expressions, thereby comprehensively capturing inconsistency information. Furthermore, we specifically design quality-aware multi-channel fusion method to further enhance the reliability of sarcasm detection results. Extensive experiments and in-depth analyses on publicly available benchmark datasets for multimodal sarcasm detection demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: S2C-Net: SAM2-Based Dual-Domain Feature Reconstruction and Semantic Decoupling for Tiny Remote Sensing Object Counting
item.dc:creator: Hailong Ning
Haojie Li
Wuxia Zhang
Tao Lei
Yanping Chen
Xiaopeng Cao
Asoke K. Nandi
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing object counting (RSOC) is a fundamental task with significant potential applications in urban traffic monitoring, road planning, and public security. However, accurate counting remains challenging since tiny objects are easily confused with complex backgrounds in remote sensing (RS) scenarios. Recent vision foundation models (VFMs) have brought new opportunities to RSOC owing to their powerful ability in learning general representations. Nevertheless, VFMs cannot be directly applied to RSOC because of significant dual-domain gaps in both task and data. To address these challenges, this study proposes a SAM2-based dual-domain feature reconstruction and semantic decoupling network (S2C-Net) for RSOC. To bridge the dual-domain gaps between SAM2 and RSOC, a difference-guided spatial-frequency feature reconstruction strategy (D-SFFR) is designed. The D-SFFR strategy adapt the SAM2 encoder to the RSOC task by reconstructing spatial and frequency domain features at critical layers. To improve the countability, a semantic object decoupling module (SODM) is further introduced for alleviating semantic confusion between tiny objects and background noise. By constructing a mutually exclusive mapping between object and noise in the semantic space, the SODM can explicitly disentangle object semantics and background semantics. In addition, a multi-scale adaptive feature fusion strategy is employed to integrate semantic and structural information across different scales, thereby enhancing the discrimination and representation capability for dense tiny objects. Extensive experiments conducted on several challenging RSOC benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed S2C-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in counting performance. The code is available at https://github.com/xuptLHj/S2C-Net. Published: 2026-05-15T19:53:34+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hailong Ning; Haojie Li; Wuxia Zhang; Tao Lei; Yanping Chen; Xiaopeng Cao; Asoke K. Nandi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3693659

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Remote sensing object counting (RSOC) is a fundamental task with significant potential applications in urban traffic monitoring, road planning, and public security. However, accurate counting remains challenging since tiny objects are easily confused with complex backgrounds in remote sensing (RS) scenarios. Recent vision foundation models (VFMs) have brought new opportunities to RSOC owing to their powerful ability in learning general representations. Nevertheless, VFMs cannot be directly applied to RSOC because of significant dual-domain gaps in both task and data. To address these challenges, this study proposes a SAM2-based dual-domain feature reconstruction and semantic decoupling network (S2C-Net) for RSOC. To bridge the dual-domain gaps between SAM2 and RSOC, a difference-guided spatial-frequency feature reconstruction strategy (D-SFFR) is designed. The D-SFFR strategy adapt the SAM2 encoder to the RSOC task by reconstructing spatial and frequency domain features at critical layers. To improve the countability, a semantic object decoupling module (SODM) is further introduced for alleviating semantic confusion between tiny objects and background noise. By constructing a mutually exclusive mapping between object and noise in the semantic space, the SODM can explicitly disentangle object semantics and background semantics. In addition, a multi-scale adaptive feature fusion strategy is employed to integrate semantic and structural information across different scales, thereby enhancing the discrimination and representation capability for dense tiny objects. Extensive experiments conducted on several challenging RSOC benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed S2C-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in counting performance. The code is available at https://github.com/xuptLHj/S2C-Net.

item.title: SSFDT: Spatial-spectral-frequency dual transformer for hyperspectral image denoising
item.dc:creator: Yuefei Zhang
Mengying Xie
Shaojiang Deng
Xiaowei Yang
item.description: Hyperspectral image (HSI) denoising aims to efficiently address the challenges of spatial–spectral coupling structures of HSIs and the decoupling of clean and noisy components. Although transformers have shown promise in capturing long-range dependencies in HSI denoising, existing transformer-based methods overlook the spatial–spectral coupling structures inherent in HSIs. This oversight can diminish the effectiveness of leveraging 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity. Additionally, they pay insufficient attention to detailed information within localized regions, resulting in unstable decoupling of noise and clean components. To tackle these limitations, we propose a novel spatial–spectral-frequency dual transformer (SSFDT) for HSI denoising. Initially, a spatial–spectral transformer (SST) module is designed to effectively capture the 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity by disentangling the spatial–spectral coupling structures inherent in HSIs. Subsequently, the spatial–spectral frequency-domain-based filter (SSFDF) is proposed to jointly capture 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity and local structural correlations in the frequency domain, thereby reducing detailed information loss during noise removal. Finally, SST and SSFDF are integrated via a dual structure to enable holistic global–local context modeling across spatial and frequency domains, fully leveraging their complementary structural characteristics for HSI denoising. Extensive experiments on various HSIs demonstrate that our proposed SSFDT significantly outperforms competitive comparison methods. Published: 2026-05-15T15:27:13+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.779 (must_read)

Authors: Yuefei Zhang; Mengying Xie; Shaojiang Deng; Xiaowei Yang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113894

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Hyperspectral image (HSI) denoising aims to efficiently address the challenges of spatial–spectral coupling structures of HSIs and the decoupling of clean and noisy components. Although transformers have shown promise in capturing long-range dependencies in HSI denoising, existing transformer-based methods overlook the spatial–spectral coupling structures inherent in HSIs. This oversight can diminish the effectiveness of leveraging 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity. Additionally, they pay insufficient attention to detailed information within localized regions, resulting in unstable decoupling of noise and clean components. To tackle these limitations, we propose a novel spatial–spectral-frequency dual transformer (SSFDT) for HSI denoising. Initially, a spatial–spectral transformer (SST) module is designed to effectively capture the 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity by disentangling the spatial–spectral coupling structures inherent in HSIs. Subsequently, the spatial–spectral frequency-domain-based filter (SSFDF) is proposed to jointly capture 3-D spatial–spectral non-local self-similarity and local structural correlations in the frequency domain, thereby reducing detailed information loss during noise removal. Finally, SST and SSFDF are integrated via a dual structure to enable holistic global–local context modeling across spatial and frequency domains, fully leveraging their complementary structural characteristics for HSI denoising. Extensive experiments on various HSIs demonstrate that our proposed SSFDT significantly outperforms competitive comparison methods.

item.title: Towards arbitrary-scale image super-resolution with prompting and diffusion prior
item.dc:creator: Guangyuan Li
Xinyue Tu
Zhanjie Zhang
Wei Xing
Lei Zhao
Yuanxing Liu
item.description: Arbitrary-scale image super-resolution methods have achieved impressive results. However, they struggle to reconstruct detailed texture information when the upsampling factor is greater than 4 × " role="presentation"> × × . To mitigate this issue, we propose a novel approach combining prompting and diffusion prior for arbitrary-scale image super-resolution, named ProDiff. Specifically, we introduce a diffusion prior generation network to learn diffusion priors that conform to the distribution of high-resolution image detail information. Next, a prior fusion module is designed to integrate diffusion priors into the backbone. Additionally, we design a scale-aware prompt block, which generates input-conditioned prompts based on images with varying degrees of degradation, then merges these prompts with corresponding scale information to dynamically enhance the backbone’s ability to represent details. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that ProDiff achieves SOTA performance in arbitrary-scale super-resolution tasks. Codes are released at https://github.com/GuangYuanKK/ProDiff . Published: 2026-05-15T04:11:02+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guangyuan Li; Xinyue Tu; Zhanjie Zhang; Wei Xing; Lei Zhao; Yuanxing Liu

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116204

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Arbitrary-scale image super-resolution methods have achieved impressive results. However, they struggle to reconstruct detailed texture information when the upsampling factor is greater than 4 × " role="presentation"> × × . To mitigate this issue, we propose a novel approach combining prompting and diffusion prior for arbitrary-scale image super-resolution, named ProDiff. Specifically, we introduce a diffusion prior generation network to learn diffusion priors that conform to the distribution of high-resolution image detail information. Next, a prior fusion module is designed to integrate diffusion priors into the backbone. Additionally, we design a scale-aware prompt block, which generates input-conditioned prompts based on images with varying degrees of degradation, then merges these prompts with corresponding scale information to dynamically enhance the backbone’s ability to represent details. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that ProDiff achieves SOTA performance in arbitrary-scale super-resolution tasks. Codes are released at https://github.com/GuangYuanKK/ProDiff .

item.title: SToRe3D: Sparse Token Relevance in ViTs for Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Sandro Papais
Lezhou Feng
Charles Cossette
Lingting Ge
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) enable strong multi-view 3D detection but are limited by high inference latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Existing sparsity methods, designed mainly for 2D vision, prune or merge image tokens but do not extend to full-model sparsity or address 3D object queries. We introduce SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries while storing filtered features for reactivation. Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content and preserve other embeddings. Evaluated on nuScenes and our new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark, SToRe3D achieves up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, establishing real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents. Published: 2026-05-13T20:53:03+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sandro Papais; Lezhou Feng; Charles Cossette; Lingting Ge

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Vision Transformers (ViTs) enable strong multi-view 3D detection but are limited by high inference latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Existing sparsity methods, designed mainly for 2D vision, prune or merge image tokens but do not extend to full-model sparsity or address 3D object queries. We introduce SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries while storing filtered features for reactivation. Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content and preserve other embeddings. Evaluated on nuScenes and our new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark, SToRe3D achieves up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, establishing real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents.
